notes.anchor: If you are in good health, it is well; I myself am in good health
notes.note: The formulaic Latin opening of an official dispatch (S.V.V.B.E.E.Q.V. = si vales bene est, ego quidem valeo), reserved for letters to the magistrates and Senate rather than to friends.

A dispatch publice was an open, on-the-record report to the Senate and magistrates, as distinct from a private letter; Cicero is careful about the line, since the Parthian war fell partly in Bibulus's province of Syria, not his own.
Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus (consul 59 BC, Caesar's obstructed colleague), now proconsul of Syria and Cicero's neighbour in the eastern command against the Parthians.
Ruler of Commagene, a small client kingdom on the upper Euphrates; his envoys gave Cicero the first report of the Parthian crossing.
A Cilician highland dynast of the Amanus, counted Rome's most loyal ally beyond the Taurus; later founder of a local royal line.

notes.anchor: Pacorus, son of Orodes the king of the Parthians
notes.note: The Parthian crown prince, son of King Orodes II (the victor of Carrhae); he commanded the invasion force and was killed at Gindarus in 38 BC.

notes.anchor: Iamblichus, the phylarch of the Arabs
notes.note: A phylarch is a tribal chieftain; Iamblichus headed the Arabs of Emesa and was reckoned friendly to Rome.

notes.anchor: which carry the revenues of the Roman people
notes.note: The vectigalia: the tax-revenues of the eastern provinces, the chief reason Rome held them and the stake Cicero presses on the Senate to defend.
Tetrarch and king in Galatia, Rome's most reliable client-king in Asia Minor, whose goodwill and troops Cicero counts as effectively his own.

notes.anchor: the rest of the kings and princelings
notes.note: The reges tyrannique: the patchwork of client kings and petty dynasts of the Anatolian interior, on whose levies and loyalty Cicero says little reliance can be placed.
